title: Delay Will Not Help Now; Rise at Once, My Brother!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source was used to resolve unambiguous OCR corruption in the interview reference, the date 11 March 2014, several broken words, and the legal passage concerning government property and advocate Bhavani Singh. The printed salutation and only source-confirmed or unambiguous repairs are restored in the Tamil reading copy. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: For the sixteenth Lok Sabha general election, the constituencies in which the DMK will contest as part of the Democratic Progressive Alliance led by it, and the candidates who will contest those constituencies, have been announced after due interviews. As always, when I finished reading the list of DMK candidates to reporters and came outside, my heart was not with me. You, who know me completely, will understand the reason. Yes, this time I could not fulfil the wishes of some senior Udanpirappugal who have laboured selflessly for the party over many years. Though I had told them when they approached me that I would fulfil their wishes, I was forced to consider the spirit of our alliance with friendly parties and what lay in their hearts.

For example, younger brother Duraimurugan is with me twenty-four hours a day. He is a senior member of the party, one of its Deputy General Secretaries, and served ably as a senior minister. When we prepared the list of people who should contest for the DMK, he sat with me, the General Secretary and the Treasurer and helped select candidates for every constituency. When Vellore party members expressed the wish that the Vellore seat be given to his son, I could not reject it. But the Indian Union Muslim League, which has been allied with us for many years, had continuously contested that seat. Professor Kader Mohideen, its Tamil Nadu president, asked that its present MP be allowed to contest again, and we could not refuse. Indeed, Professor Kader Mohideen can scarcely be described as belonging to another party. He is a person of noble character who has shared every joy and sorrow of the DMK. Only I know the pain I endured in deciding that constituency.

At a public meeting on Chennai's Marina on 10 February 1977, I announced the names of DMK candidates for twenty-four Lok Sabha seats. Murasoli Maran's name was read out for Central Chennai; party comrades printed posters and began election work. Then, on 15 February, Neelam Sanjiva Reddy came to Chennai on behalf of the Janata Party, met me and sought an electoral understanding. The Janata Party wanted P. Ramachandran to contest Central Chennai, placing the agreement in doubt. Though Navalar, then General Secretary, called Maran aside and told him not to withdraw after his candidature had been announced, Murasoli Maran felt that an alliance should not fail because of his own position. He issued a statement saying he would not contest Central Chennai and asking party members to cooperate. That old episode played across my mind.

Every constituency required deliberation of that kind. Another was Thanjavur, a parliamentary constituency I could almost call my own district. Everyone who wished to contest there was important. Ultimately, we had to choose one among three: younger brother T. R. Baalu, who had been a minister in the United Progressive Alliance government during the previous Parliament and leader of the DMK Parliamentary Party; younger brother Palanimanickam, who had been Union Minister of State for Finance; and Alagu Thirunavukkarasu, who had served as a senior minister in the AIADMK cabinet, later left that party because he disliked its conduct, joined us and has since performed excellent party work in the region. How were we to decide? If we selected one, how could we face and console the other two? Yet are not Duraimurugan, T. R. Baalu and Palanimanickam all my beloved younger brothers, people I nurtured?

After I announced the candidates, T. R. Baalu went to Thanjavur and proceeded directly to Palanimanickam's home, where he draped him with a shawl. Palanimanickam welcomed and embraced him, draped a shawl over Baalu in return, and that very evening convened a district activists' meeting at which everyone pledged to work for T. R. Baalu's victory. Watching this on television, I felt joy and a leap in my heart: "My brothers are indeed my brothers."

Similarly, younger brother Suba. Thangavelan, who has rendered invaluable service for many years as Ramanathapuram district secretary, sought permission for his son Sampath to contest. In Tiruvannamalai, former minister Ku. Pichandi sought permission for his brother Ku. Karunanidhi. In Coimbatore, Pongalur Palanisamy requested a seat for his son Paindhamizh Pari. Likewise, though Dindigul I. Periyasamy, Villupuram Ponmudy and Tiruvannamalai E. V. Velu had sons prepared to contest, they understood my instinct and did not even submit applications.

In several other constituencies, nine sitting MPs who had performed well could not be given another opportunity. Many wanted the selected candidates to be new faces and young people. We also considered giving women greater importance. We stayed awake all night, scrutinised every constituency with the greatest care, selected the candidates and announced them. You possess both the duty and the right to turn every one of them into a winning candidate.

Headquarters has also appointed coordinators to assist in properly supervising election work in each constituency and bringing everyone together in action. Teams of advocates will be formed for every constituency. I ask you affectionately to work with enthusiasm and a spirit of unity in which heart and face are one; wash away personal resentment; trust that if an opportunity did not come this time, it will certainly come next time; remember that opportunities can arrive at any moment and that leading the party along the path of victory is what matters now.

A committee comprising T. R. Baalu, T. K. S. Elangovan, poet Kanimozhi, Sarguna Pandian, A. Raja, Professor Ramasamy and Subbulakshmi Jagadeesan prepared the DMK election manifesto. After having it read to us, I, the General Secretary, the Treasurer and the Deputy General Secretary made the appropriate revisions, and it too has been released. Many who have read it are praising it as a constructive manifesto.

On behalf of the opposing front, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa uses the facilities available to her to fly from place to place by aeroplane and helicopter, gives no one else an opportunity on the stage, reads speeches prepared for her without changing a letter, and leaves. Every day, we read the few newspapers bold enough to report how people are gathered for her meetings, what happens in the towns where they are held and what ordinary people there think. The stage on which Amma speaks is said to cost ₹85 lakh. One person is reportedly given the contract to build the stage in every town; he receives ₹85 lakh and erects the same design everywhere. Men attending the meeting are said to receive either trousers or a dhoti, ₹100 in cash and a "biryani packet" for lunch. Women reportedly receive a sari instead of trousers or a dhoti. Even the arches erected along the Chief Minister's route are said to cost several lakh rupees. Is there not said to be an Election Commission? It shows keen interest in stopping even small traders carrying modest sums for business, subjecting them to searches and freezing their money. Could it not show some interest in actions such as these too?

Previously, on the stages where Amma spoke, only the candidate would stand with folded hands for the hour or more that she spoke. Other ministers and party leaders sat below the stage. This time ministers and leaders have been permitted to sit on it. That is indeed good progress. Similar compassion might be shown to the candidate forced to stand for more than an hour.

The Chief Minister spends three quarters of her speech abusing the Congress party that governed at the Centre and the remaining quarter attacking the DMK. In particular, when she spoke, forgive me, read, at Chidambaram on 11 March 2014, she lashed out fiercely at party Treasurer Thalapathi M. K. Stalin. At the very beginning, she asked: "The DMK was part of the Union Cabinet for nearly seventeen years. Did it do anything for Tamil Nadu?" She has repeated this question at every meeting from the first day of her campaign. We have already published a full-page Murasoli advertisement answering it by listing what the DMK accomplished for Tamil Nadu while participating in the Union Government. The DMK election manifesto itself begins with a detailed list of the projects we requested and secured for Tamil Nadu during those years.

Should a Chief Minister, the leader of a party, not pay attention to the reply from the opposing side to the questions she asks and the accusations she makes? If she does not care what answer she receives, but reads every day exactly what she read on the first, like the proverbial parrot that repeats the same phrase, will listeners not mock her?

At Chidambaram, Amma proclaimed, imagining that she had thought very deeply, that I had made Treasurer M. K. Stalin file a case in the Madras High Court seeking to cover everything in Tamil Nadu resembling two leaves; and that, if so, would Stalin petition to cut off everyone's hands because the Congress symbol was a hand? Every newspaper published this prominently. She further said: "The proverb says everything looks yellow to a jaundiced eye. Likewise, DMK members frightened by the AIADMK see 'two leaves' wherever they look and lament without cease."

In villages, people use a saying for someone kept merely to make up the numbers. Playing that role in defence of Jayalalithaa, Dinamani's editorial today, 13 March 2014, asks: "Merely because the Chief Minister's image has been removed from government vehicles, free goods and fair-price commodities, can the face of a State Chief Minister be erased from every voter's mind?" Will people vote for her merely because her photograph appears on those objects? Why, then, does Dinamani, the AIADMK's unofficial official newspaper, think the photograph must be there?

Should those who prepare a Chief Minister's speech not learn the facts first? If they prepare it wrongly, where can they hide their faces when the opposition later explains the truth? Did M. K. Stalin really file a case asking that everything in Tamil Nadu resembling two leaves be covered? He approached the Madras High Court seeking removal, for violating the election code, of AIADMK election symbols and Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's photographs appearing on government water bottles, government buses and public property.

No one is concerned if they place two-leaves symbols throughout the State at their own expense. The question is why the ruling party's symbol should be advertised at government expense, using taxpayers' money. No one in the DMK is afraid of their two-leaves symbol. In truth, no one in the DMK laments at the sight of it. Tamil Nadu knows well that Jayalalithaa laments each day wondering what the outcome of the Bengaluru assets case will be. To prevent that judgment from arriving quickly, she pursued litigation all the way to the Supreme Court to stop government advocate Bhavani Singh, who was meant to argue against her, from being removed. The people of Tamil Nadu now laugh as they watch the same Bhavani Singh seek an adjournment before the special court on "medical grounds" when final arguments are due.

How firmly did the national Left communist parties wait to ally with and contest alongside the AIADMK? At the last moment, Amma tripped them, deceived them and abandoned them without support. Will the people of Tamil Nadu so quickly forget her treacherous history of first winning the trust of every past ally, then deceiving them and leaving them stranded in the street? Even if they have forgotten, we bear the responsibility and duty of reminding them.

Treasurer Stalin begins his election campaign today. He has announced a campaign without a single day's rest until polling ends. Party speakers, allied-party leaders and artists in our movement will follow him with announced tour programmes. Though neither I nor the General Secretary can travel constituency by constituency every day to the same extent, we will attend campaign meetings wherever our health permits. We shall also communicate our views through "Udanpirappu" letters such as this.

Udanpirappē, do not remain indifferent thinking, "This election will not change Tamil Nadu's government; it is only a parliamentary election." Remember that the victory we secure now will be the forerunner of the 2016 Assembly election. With affection, with rightful intimacy, and with the love of your Anna, I ask you to begin today and labour tirelessly in the rare endeavour to secure an immense victory for our alliance everywhere. Delay will not help now; rise at once, my brother!
With affection,
M. K.
14 March 2014
